section.title: The Covenant Cut
section.sectionRationale: After the rescue of Lot and the meeting with Melchizedek, Abram is left with a promise but no child. Here God binds Himself by oath to the childless man, and Abram's bare trust is counted as righteousness. This is the theological heart of the Abraham story.
section.thesis: God answers Abram's fear and doubt not with proof he can grasp but with His own word and His own oath; Abram believes, and God credits it to him as righteousness, then cuts a covenant He alone will keep.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the promise of a son and Abram's faith counted as righteousness; the promise of the land questioned; and the covenant ceremony where God alone passes between the pieces.

units.label: The word, the shield, and the reward

verses.text: After these events, the word of the LORD came to Abram in a vision: “Do not be afraid, Abram. I am your {{shield|shield}}, your very great reward.”
But Abram replied, “O Lord GOD, what can You give me, since I remain childless, and the heir of my house is Eliezer of Damascus?”
Abram continued, “Behold, You have given me no offspring, so a servant in my household will be my heir.”
Then the word of the LORD came to Abram, saying, “This one will not be your heir, but one who comes from your own body will be your heir.”
And the LORD took him outside and said, “Now look to the heavens and count the stars, if you are able.” Then He told him, “So shall your offspring be.”
Abram {{believe|believed}} the LORD, and it was credited to him as {{righteousness|righteousness}}.
The LORD also told him, “I am the LORD, who brought you out of Ur of the Chaldeans to give you this land to possess.”
But Abram replied, “Lord GOD, how can I know that I will possess it?”
And the LORD said to him, “Bring Me a heifer, a goat, and a ram, each three years old, along with a turtledove and a young pigeon.”
So Abram brought all these to Him, split each of them down the middle, and laid the halves opposite each other. The birds, however, he did not cut in half.
And the birds of prey descended on the carcasses, but Abram drove them away.

units.label: The darkness and the word of things to come

verses.text: As the sun was setting, Abram fell into a deep sleep, and suddenly great terror and darkness overwhelmed him.
Then the LORD said to Abram, “Know for certain that your descendants will be strangers in a land that is not their own, and they will be enslaved and mistreated four hundred years.
But I will judge the nation they serve as slaves, and afterward they will depart with many possessions.
You, however, will go to your fathers in peace and be buried at a ripe old age.
In the fourth generation your descendants will return here, for the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete.”

units.label: God alone passes between the pieces

verses.text: When the sun had set and darkness had fallen, behold, a smoking firepot and a flaming torch appeared and passed between the halves of the carcasses.
On that day the LORD made a {{covenant|covenant}} with Abram, saying, “To your descendants I have given this land, from the river of Egypt to the great River Euphrates,
the land of the Kenites, Kenizzites, Kadmonites,
Hittites, Perizzites, Rephaites,
Amorites, Canaanites, Girgashites, and Jebusites.”

shield.gloss: A shield, that which protects and covers. God does not merely give Abram a shield; He says I am your shield. The same root lies behind the reward language, for God Himself, not the plunder Abram had just refused, is his defense and his wealth.

believe.gloss: To trust, to rely on, to regard as firm and sure. Our word amen comes from this root. Abram is not achieving anything; he is leaning his whole weight on what God has said. This is the faith that receives, not earns.

righteousness.gloss: Righteousness, a right standing before God. It was credited or reckoned to Abram, an accounting term for something placed to his account. Paul builds his whole doctrine of justification by faith on this verse, Romans 4:3.

covenant.gloss: A binding covenant, often ratified by cutting animals in two. The idiom to cut a covenant pictures the parties saying, let this be done to me if I break faith. Here God alone passes through, taking the whole obligation on Himself.

basic.context: Abram has a promise but still no child, and he is afraid. God comes to him in a vision and answers the two great questions hanging over him: will he have a son, and will his family have a land. The key question the chapter presses is simple: how does a person get right with God?
basic.hermeneutics: This is real history told with care, and one verse in it, verse 6, becomes a foundation for the whole New Testament. The main thing to see is that Abram is counted righteous before he does anything, simply by trusting God's promise. The debate is over what that crediting means, but the plain point is that righteousness comes by faith.
basic.originalLanguages: God calls Himself Abram's shield, the word for the cover that protects a soldier. Abram believed, from the root that gives us amen, meaning he leaned his weight on God's word. That faith was credited, an accounting word, to him as righteousness, a right standing with God.
basic.typology: The covenant is cut by dividing animals, and God alone, pictured as a smoking firepot and flaming torch, passes between the pieces. He takes on Himself the whole penalty for breaking the covenant. That points to the cross, where God in Christ bears the curse His people deserved.
basic.christ: Paul quotes verse 6 to show that we are justified the same way Abram was, by faith, not by works, Romans 4. And the God who alone walks the bloody path foreshadows Jesus, who alone dies to keep a covenant we could never keep.
basic.god: God is a shield to the fearful, a rewarder who gives Himself, and a covenant keeper who binds Himself by oath. He stoops to a doubting man's how can I know and answers not with a rebuke but with a solemn promise.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 4 and Galatians 3 make this chapter the charter of justification by faith. Hebrews 6 speaks of God confirming His promise with an oath. Acts 7 recalls the four hundred years of affliction foretold here.
basic.oneStory: This is where the gospel of grace is written into the story of Abraham. God promises, Abram believes, and God counts him righteous, then swears an oath He alone will keep. The whole line runs to Christ, the true offspring, and to all who share Abram's faith.

context.literary: The chapter falls into two matched halves, each opening with a promise, meeting a question from Abram, and closing with a divine assurance: first the promise of a son and the son of faith, then the promise of the land sealed by covenant. The vision and the deep sleep frame the drama.
context.historical: For Israel, freshly redeemed from Egypt, the four hundred years of bondage and the exodus with many possessions were their own story foretold to their father. The list of nations and the river boundaries described the land they were about to enter.
context.canonical: Genesis 15:6 is quoted more than any other Old Testament verse in the argument for justification by faith. The covenant ceremony stands behind later covenant theology and, ultimately, the new covenant in Christ's blood.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that Abram is reckoned righteous by faith alone, and that the land and offspring promises rest not on Abram's performance but on God's sworn, self-maledictory oath.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes how God dealt with Abram, but verse 6 is taken by the New Testament as a pattern that prescribes how all are justified: by believing God, not by works.

debates.question: What does it mean that faith was credited to Abram as righteousness?

views.label: Imputed righteousness
views.case: God reckons to the believer a right standing he does not possess in himself, received by faith; Paul reads it this way in Romans 4, contrasting faith with works and wages.

views.label: Faith itself counted as the righteous act
views.case: Some read the crediting as God accepting Abram's trust as itself a righteous response, honoring the relationship rather than a legal transfer.

debates.bearing: The first view grounds assurance in God's gift rather than the quality of our faith; both agree Abram is accepted before and apart from any obedience, which is the passage's main claim.

study.originalLanguages: God names Himself Abram's magen, his shield, protection and covering in one word. Abram he'emin, from aman, the root of amen, meaning he treated God's word as firm and rested on it. That faith was chashab, reckoned or credited like an entry in a ledger, to him as tsedaqah, righteousness. And the covenant is literally cut, karath berith, animals divided so the parties pass between them; here God alone, the smoking firepot and flaming torch, walks the path of the oath.

typology.type: The pieces of the covenant and God passing alone between them
typology.fulfillment: God takes the covenant curse upon Himself, fulfilled at the cross where Christ bears the penalty for a broken covenant.

typology.type: Abram justified by faith apart from works
typology.fulfillment: The believer justified through faith in Christ, Romans 4:23-25, the pattern of the gospel.

typology.type: The affliction in a foreign land and the exodus with possessions
typology.fulfillment: The exodus redemption, itself a shadow of the greater redemption Christ accomplishes for His people.

study.christ: Genesis 15:6 is the seed of the gospel of grace: Abram is declared righteous by believing, and Paul says the words were written for us also, who believe in Him who raised Jesus from the dead, Romans 4:24-25. The covenant ceremony deepens this: when God alone passes between the divided animals, He pledges His own life against covenant failure. At the cross, God in Christ bears that self-maledictory oath, keeping the covenant His people could not and taking their curse, Galatians 3:13-14.
study.god: God reveals Himself as shield and exceeding great reward, giving not merely gifts but Himself. He is patient with fear and with a doubting how can I know, answering with an oath rather than a rebuke. Above all He is the covenant-keeping God who binds Himself, taking the whole weight of the promise onto His own shoulders while Abram sleeps.

crossReferences.note: Paul's central use of Genesis 15:6 to teach justification by faith apart from works.
Abram's faith and the covenant promise fulfilled in Christ, prior to and unchanged by the law.
God confirms His promise to Abraham with an oath, so that we might have strong encouragement.
Stephen recalls the four hundred years of affliction and the judgment on the enslaving nation foretold here.

study.oneStory: Here the covenant with Abraham is formally cut, and the doctrine of grace is written into the story: God promises, Abram believes, and God credits it as righteousness, then swears an oath He alone will keep. This is the hinge from which Paul draws the gospel. The promised offspring narrows to Christ, and all who believe as Abram believed are counted righteous in Him.

quiz.q: Why was Abram counted righteous in this chapter?

options.text: Because he believed the LORD's promise, and it was credited to him as righteousness.
options.note: Verse 6 grounds righteousness in faith, not achievement.

options.text: Because he offered the animals God commanded.
options.note: The sacrifice belongs to the covenant scene, but righteousness is credited before it, through faith.

options.text: Because he had rescued Lot and refused the king's plunder.
options.note: Those acts precede this chapter, but the text credits righteousness to his faith, not his deeds.

options.text: Because he had earned it by a lifetime of obedience.
options.note: The point of verse 6 is that righteousness came by believing, not by earning.

quiz.q: What is the significance of God alone passing between the divided animals?

options.text: God binds Himself by oath, taking the whole obligation of the covenant on Himself.
options.note: In the ceremony the one who passes through pledges his life; God takes that on alone.

options.text: It shows the covenant depended on both parties keeping their word.
options.note: Normally both parties passed through, but here only God does, which is the striking point.

options.text: It was simply the customary way to seal any agreement.
options.note: The form was customary, but God walking alone is the surprising, meaningful departure.

options.text: It meant Abram had to guarantee the covenant himself.
options.note: Abram is asleep; the whole weight falls on God, not on him.

quiz.q: How does God describe Himself to the fearful Abram in verse 1?

options.text: As Abram's shield and his very great reward.
options.note: God offers protection and Himself as the reward.

options.text: As the God who will make him wealthy and famous.
options.note: God does promise blessing, but here He offers Himself as the reward, not merely riches.

options.text: As a distant judge who demands performance.
options.note: God comes near with comfort, not distant demand.

options.text: As a God who cannot be questioned.
options.note: God welcomes Abram's honest how can I know and answers with an oath.

quiz.q: What did God foretell about Abram's descendants?

options.text: They would be strangers and enslaved in a foreign land, then come out with many possessions.
options.note: This foretells the Egyptian bondage and the exodus.

options.text: They would immediately possess the whole land in Abram's lifetime.
options.note: The land is promised, but only after four generations and much affliction.

options.text: They would never face any hardship.
options.note: God plainly foretells enslavement and mistreatment first.

options.text: They would be abandoned by God in their suffering.
options.note: God promises to judge the oppressor and bring them out.

quiz.q: Why does the New Testament return so often to Genesis 15:6?

options.text: Because it teaches that a person is justified by faith in God's promise, not by works.
options.note: Paul builds justification by faith on this verse, Romans 4 and Galatians 3.

options.text: Because it gives the boundaries of the promised land.
options.note: The boundaries matter, but the New Testament's focus is Abram's faith counted as righteousness.

options.text: Because it records the first animal sacrifice.
options.note: It is not the first sacrifice, and that is not the reason it is cited.

options.text: Because it proves salvation is earned by obedience.
options.note: It proves the opposite: righteousness comes by faith apart from works.

questions: God told Abram, 'Do not be afraid, I am your shield.' What fear are you carrying this week, and what would it mean to let God Himself be your protection?
Abram was counted righteous simply by believing God's promise. Do you tend to think God accepts you because of what you do, or because of what you trust Him to do?
Abram asked honestly, 'How can I know?' Is there a place where you need to bring your real doubts to God rather than hiding them?
God alone walked the path of the covenant, taking the cost on Himself. How does it change your view of God to see Him bind Himself to keep a promise you could never keep?
God gave Himself as Abram's 'very great reward.' What are you tempted to treasure more than God, and what would it look like to find your reward in Him?

translationNotes.note: Your very great reward can also be rendered your reward will be very great; either way the LORD offers Himself, not merely a gift, as Abram's reward.
The verb rendered credited or reckoned is an accounting term; the New Testament reads it as God placing righteousness to the believer's account by faith.
The dashes in the printed text mark the boundary clause; here they are rendered with commas, from the river of Egypt to the great River Euphrates.
